title: Neural Optimization
use_for: reducing tensor transfer, dispatch, allocation, and readback cost
do_not_use_for: general project optimization without neural intent
rules: optimize tensor layout and data movement before micro-tuning kernels
separate stability fixes from throughput changes
keep optimization tied to forward, training, or tensor-runtime path explicitly
avoid hidden precision changes without intent
measure arena usage, transfer volume, dispatch count, and readback cost
procedure: Identify neural bottleneck and the tensor group it affects.
Classify it as layout, data movement, dispatch, math, allocation, precision, or update-path issue.
Check whether the source of truth is CPU, GPU, or hybrid.
Apply targeted optimization with explicit ownership and synchronization rules.
Preserve network correctness and numerical stability.
Re-evaluate throughput, transfer volume, and stability.
Keep optimized path explicit and observable.

primary_when: request is specifically about neural throughput, numerical stability, network layout optimization, GPU residency, or tensor transfer cost
request is about optimizing inference or training paths for neural systems
must_not_own_when: request is mainly about loader integration, IMGUI, or assembly parsing

solves: how to find the real neural runtime bottleneck instead of optimizing layers blindly
how to reduce CPU/GPU synchronization, full-arena transfers, and dispatch overhead
how to treat precision, stability, and throughput as separate review axes
does_not_solve: model quality improvements without runtime or numerical implications
adopting external accelerators as a replacement for local architecture

core_principles: The first neural performance question is where tensor data lives.
A full tensor-arena upload/download per phase dominates many kernel-level improvements.
Dispatch count and command grouping matter when operations are small or phase-split.
Precision changes are architecture changes, not harmless optimizations.
Diagnostic downloads must be separated from normal throughput paths.
Numerical stability should have explicit checks, thresholds, and failure policy.
constraints: Do not optimize kernels while hidden CPU/GPU transfers remain unmeasured.
Do not enable gradient downloads or full prediction downloads in training throughput measurements unless they are the measured feature.
Do not change precision, scaling, loss normalization, or optimizer math without a correctness comparison.
Do not conflate CPU fallback performance with GPU backend performance.
Do not add allocation-heavy telemetry to hot paths.
decision_rules: If GPU utilization is low, inspect residency, transfers, dispatch grouping, and CPU batch preparation before shader math.
If numerical instability appears, inspect loss scale, activation derivative, gradient norm, clipping, optimizer state, and precision conversion separately.
If batch size changes hurt performance, inspect arena resizing, reservations, and planner fallback policy.
If inference is slow for small batches, compare CPU fallback, dispatch overhead, and transfer cost explicitly.
If transformer code is CPU-side, do not attribute its cost to the GPU backend.

preferred_patterns: baseline profiles that split apply input, forward, loss, backward, optimizer, upload, download, and diagnostics
GPU-resident hot tensors with explicit checkpoint/readback paths
dirty range tracking for selective upload and download
batched or grouped command execution for linear operations
arena reservations for typical batch sizes
bounded telemetry and regression tests for numerical equivalence
avoid_patterns: full-arena synchronization after every backend execute
blocking readback used as a normal control path
precision conversion hidden inside generic upload/download
optimizer state stored outside the runtime tensor model
benchmarking with diagnostics enabled and treating it as production cost

recipes.name: Diagnose neural GPU under-utilization
recipes.when: Use when GPU backend exists but training or inference does not saturate the device.
steps: Measure time spent in input apply, upload, dispatch, download, loss, backward, optimizer, and diagnostics.
Count backend Execute calls and dispatches per batch.
Classify tensor transfers as required ingestion, checkpointing, telemetry, or accidental sync.
Check whether hot parameters, activations, gradients, and optimizer state stay resident.
Prioritize residency and command grouping before shader micro-tuning.

recipes.name: Stabilize a custom training path
recipes.when: Use when losses diverge, gradients explode, or precision changes are considered.
steps: Capture baseline loss, gradient norm, parameter range, and optimizer state behavior.
Validate loss normalization against batch and token weights.
Add explicit gradient clipping or norm diagnostics with bounded readback.
Compare CPU and GPU paths on small deterministic samples.
Only then adjust precision, scaling, learning rate, or optimizer math.

pitfalls: Optimizing tiled matmul while every phase still copies the whole arena.
Confusing planner CPU fallback with GPU speedup or regression.
Leaving gradient logging enabled during throughput tuning.
Treating command dispatch overhead as negligible for tiny batches.
Changing precision without checkpoint compatibility or validation data.
review_checklist: Is the bottleneck classified by evidence?
Are upload/download boundaries measured and justified?
Are backend Execute and dispatch counts visible?
Are diagnostics separated from production paths?
Does the optimization preserve numerical behavior or document the intended change?
Are batch-size, arena reservation, and fallback policies considered?
Is the optimized path explicit rather than hidden behind generic abstraction?

references.title: Neural GPU Residency
references.interpretation: Use this first for GPU source-of-truth, transfer, dirty range, and readback decisions.
references.why: Most neural performance failures in this domain are data movement failures.

references.title: Neural Training Runtime
references.interpretation: Use when throughput or stability depends on batches, losses, gradients, or optimizer state.
references.why: Training exposes the cost of all tensor ownership decisions.
